questions.question: Where are the physical qubits located in a surface code?

options.text: At the vertices of the lattice
On the edges of the lattice
At the face centers of the lattice
Both at vertices and face centers

questions.explanation: In the surface code, physical qubits are placed on the edges of the lattice. X-stabilizers are associated with vertices, and Z-stabilizers with faces (plaquettes).

questions.question: What is the relationship between the primal and dual lattices?

options.text: They are identical
The dual has vertices at the face centers of the primal
The dual has twice as many qubits
They have no relationship

questions.explanation: The dual lattice has vertices at the face centers of the primal lattice. Edges of the dual cross edges of the primal perpendicularly. This duality relates X and Z error detection.

questions.question: What is a 'smooth' boundary in a planar surface code?

options.text: A boundary where edges terminate (Z logical can end)
A boundary where vertices sit (X logical can end)
A boundary with no stabilizers
A periodic boundary

questions.explanation: A smooth boundary is where edges terminate, creating truncated X-stabilizers. The Z logical operator can terminate at smooth boundaries. Rough boundaries have vertices on the edge, where X logical operators can terminate.

questions.question: In homology terms, what is a detectable error?

options.text: A non-trivial cycle
A boundary (trivial loop that can be contracted)
Any closed loop
An open chain with endpoints at the same boundary

questions.explanation: Detectable errors have non-zero syndrome, meaning their chain has endpoints (not a closed loop). Stabilizers are boundaries of regions. Non-trivial cycles (logical operators) are undetectable.

questions.question: Why does a logical error require a chain spanning the system?

options.text: Because only long chains are visible
Because a logical operator connects opposite boundaries without creating syndrome
Because the decoder can only find short chains
Because boundaries absorb errors

questions.explanation: A logical operator is a non-trivial cycle that has no syndrome (closed loop) but isn't a boundary of any region. On a planar code, this means spanning from one boundary type to its opposite.

questions.question: How many logical qubits does the toric code encode?

options.text: 1 logical qubit
2 logical qubits
4 logical qubits
Depends on the lattice size

questions.explanation: The toric code (surface code on a torus) encodes k = 2 logical qubits. This comes from the Euler characteristic formula: k = 2 - χ, where χ = 0 for a torus.

questions.question: The code distance of an L×L surface code equals L.
questions.explanation: True. The distance is the minimum weight of a logical operator, which is the shortest path connecting opposite boundaries. For an L×L planar surface code, this requires L qubits.
